spec_match.rationale: The page does not actually use the new overscroll-gesture primitives. The runtime implementation is a JS touch-event handler that fakes the pull-to-reveal — the v149 CSS (`scroll-anchor-overflow-top`, `scroll-anchor-name`, `scroll-anchor-overflow: gesture`) appears only inside a `<pre>` code block. The page even admits this in prose (`falls back to a JS-driven approximation`).

interactive.rationale: The JS fallback is interactive on touch devices, but on desktop with no touch input the reveal never fires — there is no mouse-drag path, no `Trigger pull` button, no debug control. Most visitors will see nothing happen.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb says `Drag past the top — the panel reveals progressively`. On a desktop browser without touch you cannot drag past the top, so the experience the blurb promises only manifests on a touch device. No keyboard/mouse alternative is wired up.

edge_cases.rationale: No edge cases: no release-before-threshold case, no `overscroll-behavior-y: auto` comparison showing scroll chaining, no demonstration that mid-scroll cancellation is handled correctly. Refresh state always succeeds after 900ms — there is no failure path.

design_system.rationale: Variables only, no static-card. The reveal panel uses `--accent-blue` and `--accent-emerald` cleanly.

openQuestions.title: Use the actual v149 CSS primitives, gated by `@supports`
openQuestions.detail: Re-author the page so the primary path is the spec CSS (`scroll-anchor-name`, `scroll-anchor-overflow: gesture`, `scroll-anchor-overflow-top: refresh-panel`) wrapped in `@supports`, with the JS pointer fallback only active when `CSS.supports('scroll-anchor-overflow', 'gesture')` returns false. As currently written the page is mis-titled — it is a `pull-to-reveal in JS` demo, not an overscroll-gestures demo.

openQuestions.title: Add a mouse / pointer drag fallback
openQuestions.detail: Desktop visitors with no touch input cannot trigger the reveal. Add a pointerdown / pointermove path so click-and-drag past the top fires the same animation. Or add an explicit `simulate pull` button that animates the reveal so visitors can at least see what the gesture looks like.

openQuestions.title: Show overscroll-behavior chaining off vs on
openQuestions.detail: An edge case the CSS path uniquely handles: when `overscroll-behavior-y: auto` is in effect, pulling past the top scrolls the parent (bad for pull-to-refresh). Add a toggle that flips between `contain` and `auto` so the visitor sees why containment matters.

openQuestions.title: Add a `release before threshold` cancel state
openQuestions.detail: Currently a pull >100 always commits. The native pattern allows aborting by releasing before the threshold. Add a visual `armed / disarmed` state on the panel that tracks the pull distance and shows the abort path.

openQuestions.title: Add a banner explaining the flag / OT requirement
openQuestions.detail: Overscroll gestures shipped in dev trial; the page should carry an explicit `enable chrome://flags/#X to test the CSS path` notice. Currently the page silently degrades to JS without telling the user the spec mode exists.

summary: This is the weakest of the ten sampled concepts. It is titled `Pull to Reveal — Overscroll Gestures` but the runtime never references the new CSS properties — the spec snippets appear only inside a `<pre>` block. On desktop the demo also doesn't run at all. The page needs to ship the spec CSS as the primary path (with `@supports` feature-gating) plus a pointer-based fallback so non-touch visitors can see anything happen.
